title: Law Enforcement Scenario-Based Training for Safety and De-Escalation Act of 2025
short_title: Law Enforcement Scenario-Based Training for Safety and De-Escalation Act of 2025
plain_summary: This law tells the Attorney General to make a special training program for police officers. The training will teach police how to calm down tense situations, use force properly, and work better with their communities. The government will also give money to states and cities to help pay for this training for their police officers. The law requires reports to see how well the training is working.
last_action_text: Sponsor introductory remarks on measure. (CR H2059)

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Law Enforcement Scenario-Based Training for Safety and De-Escalation Act of two thousand twenty-five.

sections.title: Section 2 Law Enforcement Scenario-Based Training Curriculum
sections.text: The Attorney General must create a training program within one year after this law passes. The Attorney General will work through the Director of the Office of Community Oriented Policing Services to do this. This training program will be used with the grant program described in section three. The Attorney General must create a training program that teaches police officers about improving relationships between police and communities, keeping officers safe, helping officers handle stress, being aware of their surroundings, understanding how their bodies and emotions react to stress, making good decisions and solving problems, calming down tense situations, proper use of force including deadly force, and helping people in crisis. The Attorney General must work with professional police groups, community organizations, and defense agencies when creating and sharing this training program. The Attorney General must provide expert help and technical support to groups that want to use this training program. The Attorney General must study the best ways to do scenario-based training and keep up with the newest training methods. The Attorney General must create a certification process for groups that successfully use this training program.

sections.title: Section 3 Law Enforcement Scenario-Based Training Grant Program
sections.text: Starting one year after this law passes, the Attorney General can give grants to states, local governments, Indian tribal governments, other public and private groups, and groups that work across multiple areas. These grants help provide police officers with access to scenario-based training that is very similar to the training program created in section two. Groups that want a grant must submit an application to the Attorney General. The Attorney General will decide what information must be included in the application and when it must be submitted. One year after getting a grant, each group that received money must send a report to the Attorney General. The report must explain any good things and problems with delivering the training to police officers. The report must also include suggestions for making it easier for police officers to get scenario-based training. One year after first giving out grants, and every year after that, the Attorney General must send a report to Congress. This report must include how many groups got grants, the total number and percentage of police officers in each state who received this training or similar training, any benefits and problems with delivering this training to police officers, suggestions for improving the training program from section two, and suggestions for improving the grant program. No extra money is approved to pay for this law. The Attorney General must use money that the Department of Justice already has but has not spent yet.

sections.title: Section 4 Definitions
sections.text: In this law, community-based organization means a grassroots group that works in communities to make police more accountable and transparent, and has members and activities across the country. Professional law enforcement association means a membership group for police officers that works for the needs of federal, state, local, or Indian tribal police groups and works with civilian communities on shared interests. Scenario-based training means using live action simulations and role playing to put police officers in an interactive learning environment that copies real-life situations or teaches specific skills or techniques. State means any state of the United States, the District of Columbia, the Commonwealth of Puerto Rico, the Virgin Islands, Guam, American Samoa, the Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands, and any territory owned by the United States.
